title: Case statement mismatch with signed 1-bit reg compared to unsigned constant
body:
Below is the code that triggers the problem (`test.v`):

```verilog
module test;

reg signed in4;
initial begin
  in4 = 1;

  #1;

  $display("in4: %0d", in4);

  case (0 ? 1'h0 : in4)
    5'b0101: begin
        $display("1");
    end

    8'b000001: begin
      $display("2");
    end

    default: begin
      $display("3");
    end
  endcase
end

endmodule
```

Run the commands below to reproduce:

```bash
iverilog test.v
./a.out
```

The program prints

```
in4: -1
3
```

According to my understanding, the ternary operator should choose `in4`, because `0` is `false`.  
`in4` is a **signed** value and its binary representation is `1'b1`.  
However, when `in4` is compared with the unsigned value `8'b000001`, it is treated as unsigned (as explained here: [https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21340093/why-is-this-verilog-relational-statement-returning-true](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21340093/why-is-this-verilog-relational-statement-returning-true)).  
Thus, `1'b1` is zero-extended to `8'b0000_0001`, making it equal to the second case branch.  
Therefore, it should print `2` instead of `3`.
